speaker: interviewer
text: Dr. Max Epstein. March 10, 1995. OK, Max, you were talking about life in the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yep.

speaker: interviewer
text: I want to touch a little bit now on how that exciting cultural life that you had pre-war translated itself into the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, one of the things that I forgot to mention, of course, is that I read a lot. And today, when I look back, since I missed two years of high school-- and actually, in the city of Chicago, somebody was questioning my getting an apartment on the South Side when I was working there, because I didn't have a high school degree. But it worked out OK. I was at the [INAUDIBLE]. But anyway, speaking about the high school, since I didn't finish it, I look back sometimes and I had a wonderful school with wonderful teachers. But I really think that most of my education and most of my [INAUDIBLE] but certainly my-- my process of learning and acquiring knowledge and understanding was really, probably, through this reading period. Libraries were available. That is, some basements, some places, were always books. And I borrowed them and I read voraciously. And that's probably one of the finest periods, as far as self-education goes. And I believe it to this very day. As far as humor and life is concerned, there were a lot of humor. Sick humor. Some of it, I hate to repeat it here, and I probably could be charged with-- with probably vulgarity and obscenity if I repeated some of the jokes that were going around the ghetto. They were obscene. But they were humorous, because they were making fun of a situation. And so there was humor. There was life. I played bridge with my classmates. And I have-- there were all kinds of things were going on. And-- not always the same way. After '42, in September, there was a critical time when children were disappearing. That changed the nature of the place, became more like a camp in that sense. There were no children. Children created a certain environment of normal life. And when you don't have children-- any below 12, you don't see anybody-- that makes it a little different.

speaker: interviewer
text: A lot of cruelty among each other. Needless to say, when you-- when you position yourself and when you fight for survival, you're not going to be very nice. So there was a lot of cruelty. You asked the question-- he was asked the question about the self-government and the police-- the Jewish police. Was every Jewish policeman a bad guy?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, probably not. But let me tell you something. In one of the advertisements for looking for some special policemen, the Germans have issued-- the Jewish government has issued a request for people to come in to be policemen. And I remember-- not all policemen, mind you. Some of my best friends. But I'm talking about a particular group. And one of the requirements was a previous criminal record. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] As a positive thing, by the way, if I might point this out. So they were looking for some-- some really crude and cruel people. So that-- it-- but that was just an exception. I'm just bringing it up. It wasn't as a rule. But, you know, that-- there was a lot of that going on. People looked terrible. They all looked like-- walked around like skeletons. And that does-- does not enhance beauty. You know, it doesn't-- but on the other hand, life was still, you know, going on. And people, as I said, were making jokes and-- and having humor. And there were plays, and there were theaters, and there were music. And there were things. Not much. I'm not suggesting that-- that, you know, we had a Philharmonic full of people. No. That's-- but it was there. It was there to some extent. But there was a lot of cruelty to each other as well. And it has to be. I mean, it-- it's obvious. I mean, when you live in that kind of, you know-- positioning yourself for survival and trying to-- you know, that-- that's not an easy environment.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you personally experience any of this kind of cruelty?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not really. Not really. I usually stood-- no, no, not really. I mean, I'm not trying to suggest to you that I was under any-- nothing compared to Auschwitz, you know, with continuously under being murderously beaten and killed. But it's-- actually, the ghetto was wonderful in the sense that you lived in your own family environment. And you at least had a home. And you had friends. And you could resort to that, as bad as it was. So you could survive better. You-- numbers prove it. People survived more from the ghetto Lodz than from any other place. So-- of course, there were a lot of people to begin with. But-- but there was-- there was a lot of that. And life was not pretty, let's put it this way. But then again, it's not pretty in some places where you go today. Probably isn't pretty. I just don't go there either. So I would say that, by and large, we came all right. They came out. People still-- There was a lot of beautiful things, too. There were a lot of commitments in families, which may be lacking today. [LAUGHS] So hard times also produce some beauty. I mentioned to you last time, when it's very dark, every spark look very bright.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there are a lot of sparks of beauty and of devotion and commitment and sacrifice. My cousin-- you know, I have been in the army. I have been a soldier, as well, and I have certainly gone through camps. And I have known what danger of life could be.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I know how-- what courage is, what could courage mean. And I'll tell you something. Nothing measures up to the courage of my cousin, Lillian, who can, for two months, refuse to take anything but water in order to save her mother without letting her know and then burn up. I mean-- Because courage is an event. You know, when we talk about courage today-- about what we hear about, whether it's in civilian life or in military life-- it's always an act, an act of a few minutes. You know, you do something. It's very courageous. Sometimes-- sometimes knowingly, sometimes maybe instinctively. Whatever it is, it's courage.

speaker: interviewer
text: But it's a short. Do you know what it means to be two months without food when you're already hungry to begin with? So I think that this kind of courage-- sorry-- is-- you cannot compare it to any other form of courage. This is like a mother, you know, that, you know, a person who raises children realizes that, you know, raising children is not giving orders but to be with children and to take the good and the bad and being always there.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's that kind of a devotion that is really measured as a real devotion, not somebody being devoted by writing a nice letter or saying something nice. Same thing here. Courage is not measured by an event that happens one minute, but an event that can go on for so long.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's move on into the liquidation of the ghetto. When did that occur and how--

speaker: interviewee
text: I left on the 19th of August, 1944. The liquidation started at the end of July. It was crystal clear that we were going to be liquidated, and that everybody will be deported. We didn't know where exactly. But we had inklings that it's not going to be a good thing. And for two weeks, my mother and I went to the plant where I worked. There was a building. So we were housed there. We had to live-- leave our place because they were moving, like waves, and cleaning up the place. And they were-- and we stayed there for two weeks. And in fact, there was some talk that we may be going directly to some plant to work somewhere in Germany, which turned out to be a bluff, like all the other things. But there is one thing that did really happen. When we arrived in Auschwitz, there was no selection the first moment when we came in, which was the most crucial one. Because at that time, is when most people went to the gas chambers. And I don't know. My mother was, of course, 40. And obviously, she looked-- well, she didn't look too bad. But she was ancient compared, you know, to what they expected to be somebody be working. So that selection was important to pass. Not that the others were any good. Eventually, I'll come back to talk about [INAUDIBLE], talk about some of the things I heard about what my mother went through in Auschwitz. But anyway. So we were separated as soon as we arrived.

speaker: interviewer
text: Wait. In the ghetto--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, ma'am.

speaker: interviewer
text: You and your mother were in this one building.

speaker: interviewee
text: Plant.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then, what happened to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: And then, we were herded into the railroad cars, which were cattle cars, about 120, 150 in a car. And we went--

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any accommodations in the car, water, sanitary?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. There was a pile-- there was a barrel there, or a pail. And if you wanted to, you could use it. My mother disappeared during the-- during the arrival in Auschwitz. When I was standing there at the railroad tracks, my mother suddenly disappeared.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was frantic, you know. Where the heck is she? And she came back about five minutes later. She said she went to the bathroom, because for three days, she didn't dare to go to the pail. How long were you on this car?

speaker: interviewee
text: Three days.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there are any stops?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm sure there were stops, but I wasn't-- the door wasn't opened.

speaker: interviewer
text: What time of year was this? Summer?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. 19 of August, 1944, which was good, of course, in that sense. I presume you could die from cold, as well. Not that the heat was very pleasant either. There was a couple of windows which were open, so it's hard breathing, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when you arrived at Auschwitz, what was your first impression?

speaker: interviewee
text: I thought it was tennis courts. It was very flat, and everything was with wires. So my first inclination was, it must be tennis courts. Well, didn't take more than 30 seconds to realize, when they started racing us down, that no, we were not going to a tennis court. But it looked like it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what time of day did you arrive?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was daytime. No, it wasn't. It was night. It was [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened next?

speaker: interviewee
text: Next, they kept us together because, somehow or other, that plant got some special treatment. Because maybe there was some truth in the fact that we will be going to working somewhere in a plant-- electric plant. But I don't know. You know, when you deal in a machine that enormous, as the liquidation of a nation of a race of that many people, [LAUGHS] a whole lot of things are happening. Especially when you read about it today, you can see that, even though the remark that they were so efficient, you know, nothing is that efficient. So I don't know what happened in the process. But somehow or other, we were designated to something. And indeed, we were stayed for a day somewhere-- on the lawn, I think, we were sitting. I don't remember sitting with my mother still. And then, they came in, and they gave me the number. They punched numbers. Because once you arrived in Auschwitz, you were designated for Auschwitz, by and large, to stay in Auschwitz. You would get a number. There's only one camp in the-- in the Nazi occupation that-- concentration camp that gave numbers. I mean, tattooed numbers. Everybody had a number of some sort. But-- so that's when I got the tattoo.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did they do that?

speaker: interviewee
text: With a little needle. And it's like a pen, you know, with the injecting [INAUDIBLE]. And then-- and then about the day-- the next day, we were separated. But we already saw the crematoria. We smelled the-- the burning flesh. We could-- it's like burning a comb, like a comb. I don't have a comb for a long time, so I can't tell you from recent memory. But from old memory, understand. Oh, since you are familiar with this. Like film burning. Like burning film. It's that smell of organic matter.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were on the lawn.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we were on the lawn. We can see the-- and of course, people were talking already. And there were those who were there longer, the-- and prisoners, as well. And, you know, information gets transferred very quickly. And we realized where we were. And my mother was obviously very depressed. And needless to say, I wasn't ecstatic either. And we moved into the separate camps. I went to a labor camp. There were different-- Auschwitz is-- was divided. Birkenau. That was really the place where we lived. Auschwitz was actually the city where the plants were. And in Birkenau, they had this-- these tennis courts. They were divided there for separate camp, very flat, you know. One next to each other, separated by barbed wire. And actually, the-- the women's camp was next door to mine. And on the other side, there was a camp, which people were primarily gypsies and some others. They called it gypsy camp. That's why I remember. But actually, there were other people who were really ready to either be transported somewhere else or prefer-- probably to be eliminated. And just an example of a story that may be relevant, a classmate of mine-- I don't know [INAUDIBLE] classmate. Certainly worked with me in the telephone company. We became very friendly. I remember he had a girlfriend, and they were very nice. I remembered them. Somehow or other I remember him.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I ran into him in the camp. And he had a brother and a father in the gypsy camp, and he had the mother in the women's camp. And he was the transfer information. So he would go to the one side and scream through the wires, and they would know to come tomorrow at this time, you know, after whatever.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then, he would go to the other side and tell her mother-- his mother what is happening. And one day he comes to me and he says, you know, my father and my brother were taken away. Taken away is-- is-- is equivalent-- is, well, [INAUDIBLE]. Is equivalent to saying they were take-- you know, they are gone. I mean, they're probably gone to-- to the gas chambers, and they are not there anymore. And now he's going to tell his mother. And he decided, I'm not about to do that. I don't want to see her. I don't want to hear from, you know, her what she's going to do and say. So he-- he did something, which I considered at that time-- at that time I considered a classic suicide. What he did, he knew that if he goes to the hospital, he's going to be liquidated. Because anytime you go to the hospital, every week or so, they will select mostly Jews. There were others in the hospital, but Jews will be finished. Just [INAUDIBLE] periodically. Not that they wouldn't do it in our camp. Every Sunday, we had a selection anyway. So what he did, he walked into the clinic, whatever it was, in the ambula-- whatever it's called. First aid, whatever it was at the end. It was very well organized. And he-- they would give you a thermometer and say, go ahead and stick it in your mouth or whatever, and bring it back. So he brought himself a little hot water in a cup, whatever they had. And he stuck the thermometer in and brought it back. And he had a high fever. They took him over, and he died.

speaker: interviewee
text: So here is a-- because he didn't know. You know, climbing on the electric wires, you know, it's kind of a crude way of dying. So he chose to die like that. He preferred to do that rather than to see his mother. Obviously, you realize that it was very courageous act on one hand.

speaker: interviewee
text: On the other hand, he was only trading days because none of us really believed that we'll be in much longer than that. So I'm not so sure that he committed suicide out of-- you know, ended some wonderful life. But nonetheless, it's kind of, I think, maybe typical of the environment, the situation, the conditions, and the responses of different people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any contact with the mother during this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I talked to her over the wire. It so happens that we were separated by a passage where they brought all the people from the train, moving them into the gas chambers. So it was always difficult to talk to her because I had to scream across two wires, two sets of fences. But I did.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it was interesting that-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] so I talked to her. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] most of this was young punk, I guess. And so I always said, stop crying. You're going to be finished anyway. What are you so worried about?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think about it today, of course. I had an opportunity to find my mother and make up for it. But it was rather crude. But then again, when you are young, you are crude. So.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do in the camp? What kind of work did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, in Auschwitz, primarily they take you out. Well, I should really-- I should pay tribute to life, obviously. I mean, thank you for reminding me. The first day when I came-- when they took us out, they took us to Auschwitz, which is where the plant for this-- when they brought the airplanes that were dis-- that were disabled, and they used to take them apart for parts. They call it [GERMAN]. [GERMAN] means separation. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] It's places where you take things apart and you make parts of them. And you just collect them, and then you use them for other planes, probably, or whatever. So we were taken there. The whole group, the way we were, with that-- that factory that we came.

speaker: interviewer
text: Men and women?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just the men.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was no more men and women together. And-- and a young sergeant major came out-- Air Force. The Air Force was in charge of it. And they-- he came out, and he picked me. And he said, you come with me. We're going to take apart radios. I have a shack. We keep the radios there. Soon as I walked in his place, he says, look. He says, you and I, we're not going to survive that war. I just took you in because you're a young kid and I'm trying to save as many kids as I can. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] He would bring me every day, something to eat when I came in. But I got dysentery because I didn't take kindly to the environment and I always responded with gastrointestinal distress. So it lasted for about two weeks. He treated me exceptionally well. I wish I found him.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Leib. Sergeant Major Leib. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He would hide me. He had got a attic. He would hide me there so because the SS would-- the Gestapo-- the SS was going around looking for people whether they worked, you know, beating, whatever it was. So he would hide me in the attic so they wouldn't see me. There was some Polish workers, which were obviously antisemites, and they hated me and my guts. And-- but he used to tell me-- he didn't understand Polish, but he told me, well, forget it. Just don't pay attention. He was trying to teach me how to be-- steal things. He says, you'll never survive in this place unless you learn how to steal and trade and deal with things, because, you know, this is not a place to survive for-- for the meek. And one day, he brought a little boy-- a boy, my age. He was 17. [INAUDIBLE]. And he says, this guy's been beaten. He has-- he only knows one word, why beat, in German, [GERMAN]. And he doesn't speak any language. And I'm interested to know where he comes from.

speaker: interviewee
text: He was very curious, intellectual, extremely curious about things, Leib was. So he said, why don't you speak to him? You know, you speak languages, Max. You know? So I started, I don't know, Russian. And I started-- no way. Nothing. Well, Greek, apparently he spoke. But I wasn't. So I said, OK, I said. I'll start Hebrew.

speaker: interviewee
text: Perfect Hebrew. Better than mine. He was from Corfu, from a Jewish community that spoke Hebrew since the Inquisition. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And it was a bizarre place to sit there, the three of us at the table, I speaking to him Hebrew and translating it into German. He was a wonderful guy. What a guy.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then, my old director of my plant had swollen feet by then, couldn't get-- couldn't move. So I told Leib, you know, I have my old director of my plant. He's not going to survive. Could I bring him to work? He's says, OK. Point up to me next morning, and when I come out to pick you, tell me who it is, and I'll take him. And he did.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he brought him in, and he spent a few days. But he couldn't make it, and one day he just didn't have enough strength to get to work. And I guess they liquidated him. That's the Polish woman, his wife, that I went to see after the war, which I may get into that, to tell her about. So this is the terrible capitalist, you know, that I was not supposed to collaborate with to grow the vegetables. What an irony.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did your mother do?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother was in the camp. And then, I got dysentery and I told Leib that I cannot make it anymore. And I went to the hospital, and I went through the hell of that. Because that was the block where Mengele was operating, where all the castrated and all the twins and everybody was there. But we were there as patients. They gave us some drugs. But at the same time, every week, they had a selection. They were also very good at observing Jewish holidays. Germans were. Rosh Hashanah was the-- that was came-- that was about New Year's, September. So the eve of New Year's, they decided, have a big selection just to celebrate it. So Mengele came in and selected people and didn't select me.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I was on the other side. But then, they left us, together, for three days, before the trucks came in at night with the-- and I spent most of the time talking to an engineer who worked in my plant, with whom I became very friendly in wartime.

speaker: interviewee
text: His wife was in the-- with my mother in the women's camp. He had no children. He was older, and talked to me a lot for three days. I probably learned a lot about-- he was very highly educated, extremely intelligent man, multilingual and music and heart. We talked for three days incessantly. He talked. I listened. At the end, there was a rumor that, at 9:00, they are coming with the trucks, and they will be taken away. And he turned to me and said-- so the head of the particular place decided to keep the food until 9:00-- 10:00 so he doesn't have to give it to the guy-- these-- those who go to be, you know, gassed.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he said to me, you know, here I am, 40s. I don't know how old he was. My life is ending. Why am I so mad that he deprives me of my soup? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I think that this maybe also says something about the conditions, the human mind. And it's bizarre, obviously. It doesn't even make any sense. But it does. You have to always put yourself in the conditions-- to me it makes sense. I knew why he was mad. He was hungry. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And he was being deprived of his last-- don't we have a last meal here when we-- when we execute people? So apparently, there is something to it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And second, well, the next day, the Polish head-- which was hung, I understand, after the war-- the Polish doctor in charge decided he's going to do better than Mengele. He's going to make another selection. And he picked me. And so another 50 people were selected to go to the other way. But they sent me back to the barrack, you know. And I stayed there and waited for whatever. And there was a French-- I think a Jew-- physician. There were physicians there, working-- Hungarians, Frenchmen. I mean, from Hungary, from France. And he decided he's going to do something about it. So he did. And he removed that list. And the following week, which we knew-- he told me, Yom Kippur is coming. They're going to liquidate everybody. So he said, you're going to go back.

speaker: interviewee
text: So in front of that Polish doctor who had the decision-making, right, he convinced him to send me back. He sent me back. When I came back, I tried to go back to Auschwitz to the plant to find Leib, but [INAUDIBLE]. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I think you are going to the-- the end of the fourth tape. No? So what happened next?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, next I worked in some other place, taken to whatever places they took you. I felt pretty bad. By that time, my mother was not to be found. She didn't show up. Of course, she didn't know what happened to me when I disappeared. And then, when I came back, it was too late to make contact with her. And then, the selections would come. Every Sunday afternoon, there was a selection, Jews only.

speaker: interviewee
text: So it was very tough to survive, continuously. And I became very adept at surviving despite that Leib thought that I will never be able to survive because I am too naive and don't know the rules of this game of the cruel world. I don't know. In my own, amicable way, I managed to avoid.

speaker: interviewee
text: I had one instant, which I-- probably left me with a big scar in my-- I never-- I come-- my father was a very proud man, as you probably understand from  the-- probably to the point of a little bit annoyance and maybe a little bit exaggeration. But I probably inherited that. I was proud. I wasn't begging for anything, ever. I begged for my life once. It was a Polish man, head of the barrack, who always sat there and listened to my screaming to my mother. I had a beautiful Polish accent, always did. And he was somehow impressed by that. And in one of the selections, somebody else was marking me for-- and I turned to him, and he said, OK. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So a good accent sometimes helps. Not a proud time in my life. But then again, if it wasn't, there wouldn't be a life. We wouldn't be taping today.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I don't know. I-- it left me with a-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] well, whatever. I'm trying to bring this up. I'm trying to be not only candid, but honest. And that's not an easy thing to bring things like that up, because they are-- leave deep scars with you, and maybe deeper.

speaker: interviewee
text: I mean, what the heck. This is, compared to all the other things, it wasn't such a terrible thing. But to me, it was a traumatic thing. And so anyway, I-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I don't know where I am now. I'm sort of in this some period of time that I think I am now leaving Auschwitz, I presume. It's time to leave Auschwitz, as they say.

speaker: interviewer
text: I think we're going to end the tape here.

speaker: interviewee
text: So this is the last tape?

speaker: interviewer
text: No.

speaker: interviewee
text: I thought you had four tapes.

speaker: interviewer
text: I lied.
